title: Biocon's Kiran Mazumdar Shaw | EXCLUSIVE
text:
26 [music]
years of CNBC TV18.
>> Michelle, thank you very much for
joining in and talking to CNBC TV8 on
our 26th birthday. I'm going to start
the conversation by asking you, give us
a memory of when you were 26. What were
you doing?
>> So, first and foremost, I want to wish
you a very happy 26th anniversary. I
think you know CNBC has much to be proud
of and you know when I look at myself
when I was 26 I was just pro probably
one year into starting Bioon
and um
I often call myself an accidental
entrepreneur. So I was certainly a very
inexperienced entrepreneur trying to
learn what business was all about and
what uh you know the biotech uh
opportunities were for me because I had
to pioneer the whole sector because I
had nothing to follow. So it was a very
exciting yet challenging time for me
because of course no one took me
seriously. I had huge credibility
challenges but then that's the name of
the game and I think CNBC also must have
gone through those early difficult times
and look where you are today a very
serious highly respected business
channel.
>> Absolutely. Well uh you know when CNBC
TV8 in fact began covering uh bioon the
entire biotech space was extremely nent.
Did you ever think that the Indian
biotech story could be where it is at
say 26 years later?
>> You know when you start a sector um
obviously you take the first small step
but then everyone has a big dream. So
it's difficult to predict what the size
of that dream can be. But for me when I
look back on the last 47 years I feel
that a lot has changed. And I think I'm
very proud of the fact that I was able
to lead a revolution which today has
created uh a a bioeconomy
of uh 185 billion dollars and slated to
reach 300 billion by 2030.
>> Absolutely. Well, talking about the
business now, uh you have announced that
Bioon Biologics will now be a 100%
subsidiary of Bioon and uh that biatric
serum are going to own stake within
Bioon. Uh just tell us the rationale
behind the restructuring. Was the
holding company discount possibly one of
the key reasons for this?
>> So there were two things that basically
I guess swung it in this direction. Uh
the first of course was a holding
company discount and the second was the
perceived debt overhang
which we uh you know basically acquired
because of the acquisition debt. uh you
know when we acquired the commercial
interests of Viatric
um at Bioon Biologics,
it was done with the aim of creating a
large global enterprise, a very uniquely
positioned uh integrated bioimilars
company which we thought would be
rewarded because I think that was the
first time it was being attempted uh in
India and it was the biggest bio bioarma
acquisition ever done, you know, three
billion plus.
And um we were a bit taken aback. We
were very disappointed that we were
actually
um penalized for doing that acquisition
and we saw Bioon's stock take a beating.
uh and you know the the the reasons
ascribed were that they were very
uncomfortable about the acquisition debt
and uh coupled with the you know the as
you say the holding company discount we
saw a huge uh devaluation of the bioon
stock and that by extension
you know you know lowered the intrinsic
value of bioon biologics. So the whole
you know approach or the strategy of
saying let's take Bioon biologics public
because of this acquisition which would
have which anyway has made us into
amongst the top five biote biosimilars
leaders globally
uh was not being met in terms of our
objectives our stated objectives and we
felt it was intrinsically undervalued.
Now with that in mind we said let's look
at what's the best value accreative
step we can take and that's when we
mandated Morgan Standi to evaluate
uh all the options for value unlocking.
>> Okay. Well interestingly now you'll have
serum hold around uh 3 to 4% stake in
the in the in the company in Bioon. You
have Viatris which will hold around 6 to
7% stake as well. uh just give us a
sense in terms of what the relationship
with um such large shareholders would be
or such strategic shareholders would be
with Bioon. I mean basically um you know
when we look at all the
strategic investors like you mentioned
serum and uh and biatric I think it's
really about how long they want to hold
that shareholding in bio but more
importantly I think the business that we
are running uh which is a bioimilars
business um has a force of its own and I
don't think any of the strategic
investors really would make a big
difference to our path ahead. So whilst
I welcome their presence in the Bioon
shareholding, I don't know whether it
would really really influence the way we
are building the bioimilars business. I
think this is an opportunity for us uh
to really now create a large biofarma
enterprise which combines both
biosimilars and specialty genetics
especially in when it comes to what I
describe as diabeticity because we now
will be the only company in the market
globally to have a portfolio of
interchangeable biosimilar insulin
analoges.
complemented with a growing portfolio of
GLP1s
uh you know and and that makes us very
uniquely placed.
>> Absolutely. I'm going to get to the
diversityity opportunity in just a bit
but uh one of uh the speculations might
I say which might emerge is that serum
hold stake uh we have milin which holds
stake in the company as well and there's
always been speculation about bioon
succession plan. So would that be an
assumption that maybe the serums of the
world could possibly increase their
stake would probably approach you for
maybe even buying out Bioon?
>> I don't even think that's uh really on
the table because I think uh you know
people talk about succession planning
and I really don't think people should
worry about succession planning. I think
we have an excellent management team. I
think Bioon is professionally run. we
have an excellent uh you know board uh
board succession has been very well
managed and I really don't think uh you
know we need to really think about any
of the companies that you mentioned as
having to be considered in a succession
plan. So at this point in time I think
we are very well set up uh to drive the
business in a very professional way in a
leadership manner.
>> Okay. Uh well you also because of the
steps taken uh the bioon promoter
shareholding has fallen before below
50%. Uh any plans in the future to
probably hike it up beyond that 50%
mark.
>> You know I think uh it this whole
concept of promoters is also fast
dissipating and of course I will
continue to be the largest shareholder
in in Bioon. Um I think at 44% it's it's
a pretty hefty shareholding and I think
uh today it's really about uh ensuring
that you have good governance and that
uh you know shareholding should just be
about being rewarded through
performance. So I think I'm just like
any other shareholder who really wants
the company to do well and to be
rewarded with good business performance.
You also have this enabling resolution
of raising 4,500 odd crores. Uh have you
thought of which uh route you'll
probably undertake considering that
you've just done a QIP in June? Well, I
think we were oversubscribed when it
came to the QIP and I think we will go
the QIP route because that will be the
most uh elegant way of raising capital
and since we know that there's a huge
interest in in the Bioon stock and
especially through this announcement uh
we believe that the QIP would be the
preferred route.
>> Okay. Any timeline? Well, ASAP
>> I think Milin uh the total amount would
be around 3,600 odd crores is what you
would need. Uh so the remaining funds if
you do raise the entire 4,500 odd crores
would uh be used for debt reduction.
>> I think we've said up to 4,500. So we
will decide what that number should be
based on how much we raise at the QIP.
>> Your net debt, you were talking about
that it's at around $1 billion. what is
the timeline what is the level that
you're probably comfortable with and by
when
>> you know so if you look at where we are
today I think we are very comfortable
even today because we've reduced the the
debt uh eida ratio from 4.3x
in uh 2020 when we made the acquisition
to 2.5x today and we believe that you
know the next fiscal will reduce it
below two I think that's a very very
comfortable and manageable debt to have
and in fact for a business of our scale
and size. Uh I think that's the kind of
debt we can very easily manage. So I
think a billion dollar debt for the kind
of business we are building is a very
comfortable debt to manage and of course
as a company we will because we will be
throwing off free cash flow now going
forward given the fact that we've
retired all the structured equity debts
which will of course throw up another
300 crores next fiscal and the fact that
we are now uh pursuing this uh route of
merger where a lot of
uh you know uh the minority interest uh
calculations and the and and the kind of
related party transactions will also
free up more uh profits. I think puts us
in a very strong position. In fact, we
will through this combined entity have a
very very strong balance sheet.
>> Yeah. So this step is a precursor to a
possible merger that you all might
consider between the two companies.
There's a committee which is already set
and evaluating that
>> this is a deemed merger if you looked at
it because it's a wholly owned
subsidiary and when you have a wholly
owned subsidiary it's like a deemed
merger. The reason why it was uh not
considered a merged entity in the past
was because of the fact that you had
private equity investors who owned a
certain stake in the comp in biologics.
And if you remember, we created Bioon
Biologics a long time ago in 2008
as an entity to ring fence the
investments in uh bio similars in
partnership with biatric. So at that
time we had not created a wholly owned
subsidiary but we had created a separate
business segment a business division for
bioimilars calling it bioon biologics.
So it's a completing that full circle.
So you're coming back to it as a wholly
owned subsidiary.
>> So operational synergies between the two
companies between biologics as well as
say your generics business. Uh what are
the operational synergies and how will
it possibly impact your IITA as well as
your profitability considering that
you're going to have that 300 crores of
savings from interest costs uh going
forward as well.
>> Yeah. So at this point in time of course
I can't give you exact numbers but uh
there will be synergies in terms of
logistics supply chain procurement
um and a lot of the commercial uh
activities that uh we we are u doing
through our own presence in many markets
uh where bioon has been partnering with
companies in some of those markets. So
there will be a large number of
synergies but I also want to say that
both these businesses are very
complimentary businesses. So when it
comes to manufacturing of small
molecules and biologics they are very
separate uh kind of uh facilities but we
do have very funible facilities when it
comes to insulins and GLP1s. So that
makes it a very efficient uh system for
us. Um in terms of R&D again R&D of
small molecules is very different to R&D
of bioimilars. So those labs remain as
complimentary labs. Quality of course
can be synergized. Um but you know I
think the impact or the benefits you're
going to get of that kind of uh you know
synergizing or uh you know restructuring
is not going to be that huge because we
are a very lean organization to begin
with.
26 years of CNBC TV feel.
26 years of CNBC TV8.
Before we speak about um you know the
entire GLP1 opportunity as well as the
insulin opportunity for biosimilars in
particular there are some regulatory
changes which have taken place in the US
where I think phase 3 is not mandatory
anymore. Um how is that impacting the
biosimilar market on a competition basis
for say a company such as bioon along
with the fact that maybe there are some
concerns about price pressure as well.
So I think there is this perceived
threat that uh is being discussed uh by
media. I would like to put it on the
table that this actually is advantageous
for companies like Bioon.
Phase three waiverss are not a given.
They have to be earned. And I think when
you look at companies who have never
developed a bioimilar and taken it to
markets like the US and Europe, it is
not easy. Bioon has done it repeatedly
and is very very confident that it will
get these waiverss. For others who have
not yet started on this journey, it's
not going to be that easy. They will
catch up but it's going to take time.
Secondly, when you get these wers, it's
also about then following it up with do
you have the backing capacity to make
those in uh you know those biosimilars
at scale. I think what puts Bioon right
up in front uh is because of this fully
integrated model that we've created at
global scale and the fact that we will
now have a lot of funible
interchangeable assets so to speak
because we've got facilities in
Malaysia, we've got facilities in in um
Bangalore and uh you know we have very
large capacities installed in both these
uh sites. rightes. So I think from that
point of view we believe that um given
the fact that we have a very good R&D
pipeline we are the only company out of
India that's now got 10 bioimilars in
global markets I think we have an early
mover advantage and we have a leadership
advantage
>> how you looking at the opportunity that
Singene probably has because of say the
biosecure act being passed by the Senate
though it's supposed to be a more
watered down version this time And uh
separately you do have you know maybe
the slowing in the biotech industry the
early biotech industry in the US. Uh so
as a whole what are the growth drivers
for Singene? So, Singene actually has a
large number of opportunities
uh in terms of uh the CRDMO model and I
think the integrated drug discovery and
development and manufacturing model is
what Singene is now pursuing and I think
uh the fact that they've also acquired
the the facility in in in Bayiew in
Baltimore
uh is is another step in that direction.
it it will take a few quarters before
you start really seeing traction in in
that business but I'm very confident
that you will see very strong robust
sustainable growth uh in the years ahead
>> talking about Singene you know there
have been instances of uh selling some
amount of stake in Singene in the past
couple of years especially since 2022
u is there any plan in terms of further
stake sales in Singene or are you going
to keep your shareholding at around 5253
33%.
>> We will remain at this level because now
we have a very elegant business of
biofarma and research services. So I
think or the C DMO service I think
that's the way we look at this business.
I think it's a very easy to understand
business where we have a very strong
productdriven business and a very strong
service-driven business and I think that
makes it a very very interesting stock.
>> Okay. Now GLP1 um semiglutide is the big
opportunity emerging in Canada. Uh are
you in line for that?
>> Well, everyone's aiming to be in line
for that and so is bioon.
>> Have you applied?
>> Yes.
>> And yall are still awaiting approval.
Nobody's got approval as yet. Are you
expecting approval maybe in the next?
>> Well, I don't want to hazard a guess,
but everyone is very hopeful. Okay,
that's semiglutide in Canada. How big an
opportunity is it in Canada as compared
to say the rest of the markets? I think
Canada is a very big opportunity and
everyone's therefore eyeing that market
because that's the only country right
now that is open for business when it
comes to semagruide but of course the
emerging markets also are there as a
very large opportunity and I think uh
bioon is well positioned to address both
>> what about India because that goes off
patent in March. Yeah. So I think India
as you know we are out of branded
formulations but we are partnering with
companies to enter the Indian market as
well. I I believe that the Indian market
will be very competitive.
>> Okay. And what about LRA glutide? You've
launched that in the UK but uh you know
you have competition from the Munjarus
and the uh semaglutides of the world. So
just give us a sense in terms of how
that particular drug is doing. So
lagglutide is an interesting drug
because you know as long as both
semaglutide and munjaro are patented
drugs there's always space for a generic
uh in that in that segment um I think um
you know there are various ways of
looking at laglutide you know today
there's a lot of micro dosing of both
semaglutide and munjaro for weight loss
that is being attempted and you know
there are there's a there's a case to be
made saying that if you're willing to do
daily micro doing of these products then
why not use laglutide which has got very
solid data for weight loss if you're
going to do a daily injection. to leave
us with u mishaw a vision say a fiveyear
vision for bioon as a whole and say
maybe even a 10-year vision in terms of
where you see bioon
>> I don't think it's good to give a
10-year vision because in 10 years so
much can change
>> okay let's keep it to five years
>> but as a fiveyear vision I think uh we
would like to see ourselves as a leading
um biioarma company both in products and
services and I think in the product
segment we would like to be again very
dominant in diabetes.
We would like to have a very strong
leadership profile in when it comes to
oncology combining bioimilars and
genetics and of course immunology that
is another big area which is now
becoming bigger and bigger. You know
just these three areas accounts for 40%
of global pharma today and that gives us
a huge opportunity to grow and to lead.
>> Miss Shaw, it's been a pleasure. Thank
you very much for talking to CNBC TV18
on our 26th birthday. Thank you.
>> Thank you very much, Eka. And again, my
best wishes on your 26th anniversary.
>> Thank you. Thanks.
26 years of CNBC TV8.
